item.title: Purported Nancy Guthrie note includes unusual demand that breaks basic ransom rule: retired FBI negotiator
item.description: A retired FBI negotiator says the first ransom note in the Nancy Guthrie Tucson kidnapping case is inconsistent with a typical ransom motive.
item.content:encoded:
The latest revelations about the purported first ransom note in the Nancy Guthrie case appear inconsistent with a kidnapping for ransom, according to a retired FBI negotiator.

"The fact that they wanted to cut off communication quickly suggests that they may not have had kidnapping for ransom as a primary motive, even though that's what this says," said Jason Pack, a retired FBI supervisory agent. "It doesn't seem like how a usual kidnapping goes down."

He said typical kidnappers want to keep communication channels open to negotiate payment and maintain leverage over the victim's family, making the reported demand for no further contact highly unusual.

NEW DETAILS FROM NANCY GUTHRIE RANSOM DEMAND REVEALED, 'HER LIFE IS IN YOUR HANDS': REPORT

"The most telling sign is this no negotiation, no further contact line," Pack told Fox News Digital. "Genuine kidnappers wanna keep the line of communication open."

The sender did, however, incorporate known pressure tactics, including setting a deadline, making threats and telling the family that Guthrie knew what would happen if they failed to comply, Pack said.

But cutting off communication before the deadlines passed was also unusual, he added.

NANCY GUTHRIE RANSOM NOTE CLAIMED THAT SHE HAD DIED, BEEN ‘BURIED WITH NATURE,’ SOURCE SAYS

Guthrie was taken from her home in Tucson's Catalina Foothills neighborhood in the early morning hours of Feb. 1, according to the Pima County Sheriff's Department and the FBI. She has not been seen since, and no proof of life was ever provided.

In a second purported ransom note, the sender indicated Guthrie had died soon after she was taken and been buried "with nature."

New excerpts from what is reportedly the first note received by local media shed light on why Pack believes the communication departed from a traditional ransom demand.

NANCY GUTHRIE RANSOM NOTES DON'T MATCH SUSPECT'S BEHAVIOR, PROFILER SAYS: 'I DON'T BELIEVE THEY'RE REAL'

Briana Whitney, who was working for the Phoenix-based AZ Family news outlet when the case broke, joined "Crime Junkie" host Ashley Flowers on Wednesday to discuss the note her former outlet received. "Crime Junkie" declined to share copies of the text.

"Hello Savannah, we have your mother Nancy," she began, reading from what she said was the first note sent to local media. "She is safe but scared. She will be held for ransom, and once payment is received, she will be released unharmed. We will be holding her for a maximum of seven days."

As previously conveyed by authorities, the sender gave two deadlines with increased money demands the longer it took.

"Once payment is received to the Bitcoin address below, she will be released within 12 hours of deposit to a safe drop-off location back in Tucson," Whitney read. "If the payment is not received by the last deadline on Monday the 9th at 5 p.m., she will be killed. Your mother is aware of this, and her life is in your hands."

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

The sender left no room for further dialogue.

"It is in the best interest of everyone to have this completed as soon as possible," the note reportedly continued. "You will not be able to contact me from here on out. There will be no negotiation. Do not play games. Law enforcement will not be able to help you."

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

The note also included details that may have only been available to investigators and the suspect at the time, including the location of Guthrie's Apple Watch and details about the smashed floodlight in her backyard — but even that is not enough to pin down kidnapping for ransom as a motive, Pack said.

"The details about the security camera and the spotlight that was out, that proves that they know what the home was like, what the environment may have been like, but it's not a real proof of life," he said.

SEND US A TIP

In most ransom kidnappings, he said, the abductors will want to prove they have possession of the victim and that the victim is alive.

"All these things put together don't seem really like they go with the kidnapping for ransom," he said.

Investigators appear to be split on the notes' authenticity. While one person has already pleaded guilty to sending an imposter ransom note to Guthrie's other daughter, Annie, and her husband Tommaso Cioni, that case is not believed to be connected to the notes sent to local media stations.

"The FBI and its task force partners have received several ransom notes over the course of this investigation," the FBI's Phoenix office said in a statement in June. "Some have been deemed to be extortion attempts without legitimacy. Other ransom demands may potentially be legitimate and are still being investigated as such. This case continues to be investigated as a kidnapping for ransom case. The FBI has and will continue to offer all assistance possible in the investigation — however local authorities remain the lead."

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Some investigators said they were confident that the ransom demands are all bogus, while others are cautious about reaching that conclusion while Guthrie's whereabouts remain unknown, federal law enforcement sources told Fox News and Fox News Digital. Still, the two notes that Whitney mentioned have been deemed potentially authentic by investigators and by the Guthrie family.

The only known suspect remains publicly unidentified — a masked intruder who appeared on Nest doorbell camera video that the FBI recovered with help from Google.

He is described as standing between 5 feet, 9 inches and 5 feet, 10 inches tall with an average build. He was wearing a black Ozark Trail backpack. It remains unclear whether he acted alone.

The sum of rewards in the case is over $1.2 million.

Anyone with information is asked to dial 1-800-CALL-FBI. Tips can also be submitted anonymously to Tucson's Crime Stoppers affiliate at 520-88-CRIME.

item.title: Mamdani warning to wealthy New Yorkers about 'fair share' sparks online firestorm: 'Full blown communist'
item.description: Zohran Mamdani's viral post celebrating New York City's new pied-à-terre tax on $5M+ second homes drew sharp backlash from conservative critics.
item.content:encoded:
New York City socialist mayor Zohran Mamdani is facing heat on social media after his post celebrating the state’s new pied-à-terre tax on New Yorkers with a second home.

"If you have a second home in New York City worth more than $5 million, check your mailbox when you’re back in the five boroughs — because you've got mail," Mamdani posted on X, touting the tax on specific second properties introduced by New York Gov. Kathy Hochul that he supported going into effect soon.

"Today, we sent notification letters to property owners, letting them know that our new pied-à-terre tax is coming soon," Mamdani continued. "The best city in the world deserves the best parks, libraries, and schools in the world. That's only possible when we all pay our fair share. "

As of Friday morning, Mamdani’s post had been viewed over 10 million times and conservatives on social media blasted the mayor and warned that the exodus of wealthy New Yorkers will only continue under this new plan.

HOCHUL CONFRONTED ON STUDY SHOWING MILLIONAIRE EXODUS IN NEW YORK CITY

"Never elect a politician who: (1) sees your money as his slush fund, (2) loves Karl Marx, or (3) uses French words like ‘pied-à-terre’ to disguise new, confiscatory taxes he wants to impose to redistribute wealth," Republican Sen. Mike Lee (R-UT) posted on X.

"Socialism - Florida’s best real estate agent," Republican Sen. Ashley Moody (R-FL) posted on X.

"NYC’s pied-à-terre tax hasn’t even started and brokers already say owners are calling to sell," GOP strategist and attorney Mehek Cooke posted on X. "Empty units don’t tip [doormen] or pay supers. You’re not taxing the rich. You’re firing the working class that depended on them. "

"More incitement," Judicial Watch President Tom Fitton posted on X. "Communist @NYCMayor celebrates a confiscatory tax targeted at a select few. "

MAMDANI SETS INTERNET ABLAZE WITH VIDEO EXPLAINING CAVE ON PLAN TO ARREST NETANYAHU: 'WHAT A JOKE'

"What will the excuse be when literally nothing improves after all these tax increases?" New York City Republican Councilwoman Vickie Paladino posted on X. "Will they just demand even more, or will they tell us to ignore our own eyes as they insist they’ve actually fixed everything? Both? "

"The condescension towards the people who already pay a ton of property taxes to NYC," Washington Free Beacon investigative reporter Chuck Ross posted on X. "He thinks you're an immoral person and worthy of mockery if you have a second home. ‘when you're back in the five boroughs.’"

"That's only possible when we all pay our fair share,’ says the man who’s literally lived off of taxpayer dollars for years, produces nothing, and is concentrating the burdens on others—others who’ve contributed exponentially more to the city in both taxes and charitable contributions than he could possibly hope to contribute in 10 lifetimes, I might add," Manhattan Institute fellow Rafael Mangual posted on X.

"You can practically feel the glee emanating from this tweet so it's hilarious that people will still refuse to admit he's a full-blown communist," civil rights attorney David Pivtorak posted on X.

Mamdani’s support of the pied-à-terre tax on second homes worth more than $5 million has been a viral controversy for months and started when

In April, New York City Mayor Mamdani and Hochul unveiled the joint legislative proposal targeting luxury second homes worth $5 million or more. Known as a pied-à-terre tax, the annual surcharge is expected to generate an estimated $500 million from affluent out-of-state property owners.

Mamdani has previously said revenue from the tax would go toward initiatives such as free childcare, cleaner streets and safer neighborhoods.

Critics, including billionaire Ken Griffin whose luxury New York City home was featured in Mamdani's controversial video announcing the tax, argue the tax will only drive more wealthy New Yorkers out of the state and bring jobs and investments with them.

Fox News Digital reached out to Mamdani's office for comment.

Fox Business's Kristen Altus contributed to this report

item.title: Viral video shows massive bison popping tourist vehicle's tire in Yellowstone National Park
item.description: Viral video captures a massive bison popping a tourist vehicle's tire in Yellowstone National Park, and the hilarious internet reactions are priceless.
item.content:encoded:
An incredible viral video shows a massive bison jacking up a vehicle.

There are few things more entertaining on the internet than great nature videos. I'm not even sure I can explain why that's the case.

It just is.

WOMAN RISKS BEING GORED BY BISON FOR A SELFIE AT BADLANDS NATIONAL PARK IN SHOCKING FOOTAGE

Whether it's something beautiful or something downright terrible, almost all of it is must-watch content, and that leads us to a video currently making the rounds.

TikTok user @tonya_names recently posted an awesome video showing a huge bison popping the tire on a tourist's vehicle.

"I think this bison knew we were NDSU Bison fans so picked on the car in front of us 😳 glad we escaped the bison hit," she wrote.

WOMAN ATTACKED BY BROWN BEAR WHILE HIKING WITH FRIEND AND 3 DOGS NEAR ALASKA STATE PARK

The incident took place in Yellowstone National Park.

You can watch the incredible footage below, and let me know your thoughts at David.Hookstead@outkick.com.

The comments were also popping with hilarious reactions. One person wrote, "The Bison are done with us. They had a meeting with the Orca and decided on violence."

LET'S ALL AGREE THAT DOLPHINS ARE THE 'SMART KIDS WHO DON'T APPLY THEMSELVES' OF THE ANIMAL KINGDOM

Another added, "It saw the California plates."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

A third commented, "Popped it like he knew exactly what he was doing. PRECISE."

Another viewer wrote, "Uncle Bison owns the tire shop a mile up the road."

It's Friday. We're looking for some fun and uplifting content, and it's going to be hard to beat this video. Let me know your thoughts at David.Hookstead@outkick.com.

item.title: Trump-fired US attorney launches unprecedented court fight after just 54 minutes on the job
item.description: Roger Rogoff's lawsuit challenges whether Trump can fire a court-appointed U.S. attorney, setting up a novel test of presidential power over prosecutors.
item.content:encoded:
The Trump administration fired a newly sworn-in U.S. attorney just 54 minutes into his tenure.

Now, in what is believed to be the first lawsuit of its kind, he is fighting back.

Roger Rogoff was appointed to serve as the U.S. attorney for the Western District of Washington last week after a panel of district court judges, among them Democratic and Republican appointees, unanimously decided that he should fill the vacant role. The Trump administration fired him just 54 minutes after he was sworn in, prompting Rogoff to file a lawsuit alleging that the Trump administration lacked the authority to remove him.

TRUMP ADMIN FIRES US ATTORNEY IN SEATTLE MINUTES AFTER HE WAS APPOINTED

The legal challenge sets up a novel test of presidential power over court-appointed U.S. attorneys, pitting the Trump administration’s claim of broad removal authority against a federal law Rogoff says allows him to serve until a Senate-confirmed successor takes office. The outcome could determine whether presidents may dismiss judicially selected prosecutors at will — and potentially leave key offices under temporary leadership without seeking Senate approval.

Rogoff argues that, while the law allows the Department of Justice to appoint an interim U.S. attorney for 120 days, after that period has expired, the local district court retains the right to appoint a replacement "until the vacancy is filled," meaning that he can’t be fired until a permanent replacement is approved by the Senate.

Allowing the president to immediately remove any court-appointed U.S. attorney he opposed, according to Rogoff, would permit an administration to circumvent the Senate-confirmation process indefinitely.

"The district court did not coordinate with DOJ on this selection," a spokesperson for the agency told Fox News Digital of the firing. "Consistent with other firings of this type, this is wholly within the President’s authority."

TRUMP PICKS JAMES MCDONALD TO LEAD POWERFUL SOUTHERN DISTRICT OF NEW YORK AFTER JAY CLAYTON'S DEPARTURE

Rogoff’s firing was the latest in a string of dismissals — such as those in New Jersey, New York and Virginia — where the Trump administration has removed U.S. attorneys very early in their tenure. Rogoff is unique in that he is the first to challenge his removal in court.

U.S. attorneys serve as the federal government’s chief prosecutors in their districts, overseeing criminal prosecutions and representing the United States in civil cases. They also direct local U.S. attorney’s offices and coordinate federal law enforcement priorities.

"District court judges can appoint a temporary U.S. Attorney, and POTUS can fire them," acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said on X shortly after the firing. "[Western District of Washington] judges abandoned the time-honored process of consultation with the administration so that the selected U.S. Attorney is qualified to serve in the administration."

TRUMP DOJ FIRES PROSECUTORS TIED TO FACE ACT CASES AFTER REPORT ALLEGES BIAS AND MISCONDUCT

Several experienced federal attorneys, including many in U.S. attorneys' offices, have departed the second Trump administration, often going to work for Democratic-run states and taking north of a decade of legal experience with them.

In his complaint, Rogoff points to administration statements indicating that court-appointed U.S. attorneys who lack the president’s support will be removed.

Rogoff was appointed a King County Superior Court judge by Gov. Jay Inslee, D-Wash., in 2013 and worked in private practice after leaving the bench.

The White House referred Fox News Digital to the DOJ when reached for comment. Rogoff’s attorneys did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Bill Ackman reveals why he's not a 'fan' of Mamdani, doesn't rule out mayoral run against him
item.description: Billionaire Bill Ackman said Monday that Mayor Zohran Mamdani's policies were a disaster for New York City and discouraging development.
item.content:encoded:
Billionaire Bill Ackman called out New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani on Monday and labeled his policies a disaster for New York City.

"Money and Rehab" host Nicole Lapin asked Ackman if he was bullish or bearish on the socialist mayor.

"I'm not a fan," he responded. "He's very smart, very charismatic, very good at politics, but I think the decisions he's making are very bad for New York."

When asked if he'd consider running for mayor, Ackman said, "I would say possibly, but I think I could achieve a lot more by helping a very talented person do that job."

Ackman brought up Mamdani's video detailing a proposed tax on second homes worth more than $5 million. He filmed the video outside hedge fun CEO Ken Griffin's apartment.

ZOHRAN MAMDANI FACES BACKLASH OVER 'CREEPY,' 'UNETHICAL' VIDEO FILMED OUTSIDE BILLIONAIRE KEN GRIFFIN'S HOME

"This is an annual fee on luxury properties worth more than $5 million, whose owners do not live full-time in the city. Like for this penthouse, which hedge fund CEO Ken Griffin bought for $238 million," Mamdani said in the clip.

Griffin sharply criticized the move, calling it a "personal attack" and a "profound lack of judgment" during remarks at the Norges Bank Investment Management 2026 Investment Conference in Oslo, where he questioned what he described as the "demonizing" of business leaders.

"I think he's scaring away, discouraging. You know, you saw the whole video, I'm sure, with Ken Griffin's apartment. Ken Griffin is a massive driver of New York City economic value, about to make a massive investment in New York City, and he has this major presence in Miami, and now he's increasing the presence in Miami and diminishing the presence in New York. That's a disaster," Ackman said in the podcast interview.

He said the many things Mamdani wants to fund and pay for require the revenue of people like Griffin.

"So, if you scare away Ken Griffin, we discourage the Ken Griffins of the world from being in New York City. it's a disaster for New York," Ackman added.

NEW YORK CITY LANDLORDS WARN AMERICAN DREAM IS SLIPPING AWAY UNDER SOCIALIST RENT POLICIES

Ackman said earlier in the podcast that he hoped Mamdani could be a better mayor but that he didn't feel that socialism was the answer.

The billionaire hedge fund manager took issue with several of Mamdani's policies, including the rent freezes. Ackman said Mamdani was right to address the housing affordability crisis in the city, but that his plan to freeze rent was not going to help.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

"There are buildings in New York that are comprised of rent-stabilized units and where those people got a two-year rent freeze, and free market units where those people their rents had to go up in order for the landlord to cover their, you know, costs that have inflated because half his rent roll is fixed for the next two years," Ackman said. "I mean, that seems like an entirely unfair system."

He said it was also discouraging development in the city, and said Mamdani wasn't doing anything to increase the housing supply, which Ackman argued would bring down the cost of rent.

"Developers are risk takers, and they're the last dollar in, and they're not going to risk their capital unless they believe it's a friendly environment for landlords," he added. "New York has become a very unfriendly environment for landlords."

Ackman did praise Mamdani for starting a "DOGE-like" savings effort and said he hoped it goes somewhere.

Mamdani's office did not immediately return a request for comment from Fox News Digital.

item.title: WATCH: Trump DOJ vows to pursue ‘every single’ voting violation after NJ roll shocker
item.description: Harmeet Dhillon says DOJ will prosecute every illegal vote found, citing a "broken windows" strategy after New Jersey's noncitizen voter rolls bombshell.
item.content:encoded:
The Justice Department’s top Civil Rights official pledged the Trump administration will pursue every provable case of illegal voting under what she described as a "broken windows" enforcement strategy, marking a sharp shift from prior administrations as the DOJ intensifies scrutiny of voter rolls following revelations that noncitizens were registered to vote in New Jersey.

Assistant Attorney General Harmeet Dhillon told Fox News Digital the department's approach is to investigate every credible lead and prosecute every case supported by the evidence, rejecting what she described as the prior view that only widespread fraud or election-altering misconduct warranted federal action. The pledge follows revelations from Democratic New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill that thousands of noncitizens ended up on the state's voter rolls through the motor-voter system.

"What’s different, first of all, is the people in charge — and ‘people are policy’," Dhillon said when asked why the DOJ under previous administrations often uncovered alleged election-related wrongdoing but rarely secured convictions.

"[Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche] and I and the other fine career and political lawyers at the Department of Justice are dedicated to enforcing our federal civil rights laws. It is correct that in prior years, there has been this attitude that, unless there's some kind of wholesale fraud, or it changed the outcome of the election, it isn't going to get prosecuted, but that's not our attitude."

NONCITIZEN EX-KANSAS MAYOR PLEADS GUILTY TO ILLEGALLY VOTING MULTIPLE TIMES

Dhillon said her prosecution philosophy follows a "broken windows" strategy, originally popularized by then-New York City Mayor Rudolph Giuliani and NYPD Commissioner Bill Bratton, which focused on enforcing minor offenses to deter more serious crimes.

"We need to go after every single one of these violations … I can't talk about a lot of what we're doing publicly, but activists and others are giving us leads. Local officials are giving as leads. And we are carefully running down each of these leads."

Dhillon emphasized prosecutors will bring charges only when supported by evidence that is "100% correct."

CALIFORNIA ACCUSED OF BLOCKING FEDERAL VOTER ROLL AUDIT AS DOJ ESCALATES PROBE OF ELECTION FRAUD CLAIMS

"If one does not lead to a conclusion that that person voted illegally, and I'm glad I did my due diligence rather than shooting from the hip."

When noncitizens are found to have voted illegally, Dhillon suggested the DOJ will pursue penalties such as barring them from future naturalization.

A former vice chair of the California Republican Party, Dhillon considered whether New Jersey's figures could be extrapolated to other states, including California.

"I think there are more than 400 [illegal votes cast in New Jersey] I'm willing to bet. New Jersey is a small state. I came from California where I lived for the last 25 years. I'm going to predict if I were in prediction market mode that the figures there are orders of magnitude higher," she said.

GOP ERUPTS OVER 6,600 NONCITIZEN VOTERS FOUND IN BLUE STATE AS SCANDAL DEEPENS: ‘WORSE THAN ANYONE EXPECTED’

"And the system is frankly rigged in such a way that officials don't want know. People check off a box. Nobody checks whether people are citizens or not. And nobody checks duplicates. It's a sloppy mess in most of the United States, and we Americans deserve better."

When asked about Dhillon’s comments regarding California, an official in Gov. Gavin Newsom’s press office said the Democrat does not administer elections, count or certify results and hopes vote tabulation can be completed faster following June's marathon primary period.

Newsom recently signed the state's FY-2027 budget, which includes about $40 million for increased county staffing, technology upgrades and voter outreach, and approved a bill cutting the vote-count deadline from 30 days to 13.

Fox News Digital reached out to California Secretary of State Shirley Weber for additional comment.

Dhillon said the New Jersey situation underscores why other states should partner with the federal government and turn over their electronic voter data.

Many Democrat-led states have balked at such requests and are being sued in turn. Dhillon said the national problem lies in automatic registration policies combined with a "flood of illegal aliens."

Dhillon said the DOJ sent "election monitors" to key Arizona counties during Tuesday's primary and confirmed the agency will deploy federally trained monitors to several jurisdictions nationwide going forward.

When asked what happens at the end of the five-day window her letter gives Sherrill and New Jersey Lt. Gov. Dale Caldwell to respond with voter data, Dhillon said her staff has filed an additional pleading in an ongoing suit against Caldwell as to why turning over the rolls is necessary.

"With respect to this five-day window, we're looking for voluntary enforcement and what your viewers need to understand is that through a variety of court doctrines, there's window. After which you can't update the voter rolls," she said, noting most states' 90-day windows are closing.

Meanwhile in Trenton, state Senate Oversight Committee member Joe Pennacchio sent a letter to Blanche asking the DOJ to not only probe voter rolls but the entire motor-voter framework blamed by Sherrill for the problems.

The French vendor that provides the technology for the system pushed back on Sherrill on that front, saying the issues do not lie in their systems but in the Department of State.

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

"The People of New Jersey deserve more than empty assurances from the same government that oversaw this foreseeable failure," said Pennacchio, R-Boonton.

"They deserve an independent investigation that follows the evidence wherever it leads and provides a complete and transparent accounting of the facts so that any vulnerabilities that led us here can be fully and immediately remedied."

Asked about the new demand, Dhillon suggested it simply widens the net of scrutiny, remarking, "we'll get to the bottom of it for sure."

Fox News Digital's Hannah Brennan contributed to this report.

item.title: Anthony Fauci delivered one of the worst first pitches in baseball history six years ago
item.description: Anthony Fauci's infamous masked first pitch at the empty Nationals stadium six years ago remains a lasting symbol of the bizarre 2020 pandemic summer.
item.content:encoded:
Happy six-year anniversary to all who celebrate!

Wait, you don't know what I'm talking about? Come on! It's been six years and you've already forgotten the most miserable, dystopian, disgusting, disgraceful time period of our lives?

Forgot may be a bad word. How about erased? You've erased it from your mind. That's fair. I've tried, too. But I can't completely block out all the images from the Summer of 2020.

The Summer of George (not Costanza!).

The Summer of COVID.

The Summer of mid-week NASCAR races with empty stands, and 'Tiger King,' and daily death counters on every news station, and peaceful protests (according to CNN!).

LEGACY MEDIA IGNORES MASSIVE ANTHONY FAUCI DOCUMENT RELEASE ALLEGING HE MISLED CONGRESS ON COVID ORIGINS

The absolute worst summer of our lives, and it's not particularly close. We've all put a lot of that nonsense behind us, but one image in particular still sticks out.

Happy anniversary to, inarguably, the worst first pitch in the history of Major League Baseball, courtesy of a masked-up Anthony Fauci:

My God. It still makes me laugh to this day. It'll never get old. It'll never not be funny.

Above all, it'll never not be a reminded of just how looney-tunes the world was six years ago. The Yankees and Nationals kicked off a shortened MLB season in front of an empty stadium with cardboard people in the seats, and piped in crowd noise.

Of course, they also had to honor BLM before Fauci's disastrous first pitch. This was back when society still hadn't realized how much of a sham that operation was.

That "baseball season," we saw BLM flags and banners at every game, there were no fans, and coaches and umpires had to mask up before they argued.

Seriously:

Again, it was such a silly summer. Such a silly time. Our kids will read about it one day in the history books, and they will just laugh at us.

And we'll have to just sit there and take it. There's nothing we can say or do. We'll have to sit there and laugh at ourselves, right alongside them.

At least, though, we didn't throw the worst first pitch of all time.

That honor belongs to Dr. Anthony Fauci, who was the biggest fraud of all from that miserable time period.

Perhaps we all should've known the second he spiked the ball four feet in front of him that he didn't have a clue what he was doing?

Or, you know, maybe we should've know when he was later spotted in the stands with his mask off?

What a time to be alive:

item.title: Saquon Barkley 'can understand' backlash for his Trump hangout, 'glad' Giants stars worked out differences
item.description: Saquon Barkley reflected on golfing with President Donald Trump and how members of the New York Giants had conversations about Jaxson Dart introducing him.
item.content:encoded:
Days before the Super Bowl LIX-winning Philadelphia Eagles paid a visit to the White House to celebrate their title, star running back Saquon Barkley took it a step further.

Fresh off a 2,000-yard campaign to be named the Offensive Player of the Year, Barkley rode Air Force One and spent a day with President Donald Trump at his golf course in Bedminster, New Jersey.

Barkley received quite the blowback but defended himself at the time.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

"lol some people are really upset cause I played golfed and flew to the White House with the PRESIDENT," Barkley posted to X at the time. "Maybe I just respect the office, not a hard concept to understand. Just golfed with Obama not too long ago… and look forward to finishing my round with Trump ! Now ya get out my mentions with all this politics and have amazing day."

New York Giants quarterback Jaxson Dart introduced Trump at a New York event in May and received similar criticism. As someone with experience, Barkley learned the hard way that "you got to stay away from politics."

"You're not going to win. I played golf with the president of the United States of America, Mr. Trump, and I played with Barack Obama, and the one day I play with Trump, all these crazy things were said about me, and now I'm this person, I'm that person, and I don't align – some of the stuff that I don't even align with. But it's just, as an athlete, you know, you're not going to win. You're not going to win on that..." Barkley told Fox News Digital in a recent interview.

SAQUON BARKLEY SEES 'NO REASON WHY' NFL MUST KEEP TURF FIELDS AFTER FIFA WORLD CUP REQUIRED GRASS SURFACES

"That was a decision that I made, and I got a lot of backlash for it. I can understand why I got backlash for it. I don't think the things that were said about me is true. I know it's not true. It's a lot of stuff that — just because, you know, I don't get into, like, you got to be this way or that way. Just because you played golf with the president of United States doesn't make you this. Just because you played golf with Barack Obama don't make you this, but in the world that we live in, the chaos that we live in, you're either this or you're that. And that's just so far from the truth. But at the end of the day, I was blessed with a pretty cool opportunity."

Several teams, most notably in the NBA, have skipped out on the White House visit, but a D.C. trip for champions is all Barkley has ever known.

"You may not get that opportunity again, and it's no guarantee that we're going to win a Super Bowl again, and if we win a Super Bowl, growing up my whole life, what do you do? You win a Super Bowl, you go to the White House, you represent your team," he said.

Barkley was offered a position on Trump's Council on Sports, Fitness, and Nutrition but declined.

The criticism Dart received, however, wasn't just from the outside, but also his own locker room. Defensive lineman Abdul Carter said he thought the video of them two was "AI," adding, "What are we doing man?" Carter and Dart told reporters they discussed the matter and even hugged it out after they spoke to the media about the ordeal.

Barkley said he even talked to Dart about the situation and praised the Giants stars for having conversations with one another.

"I'm glad him everything that went over there in New York, they put that in the past and get back to ball," Barkley said.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: Playboy's celebrity-driven relaunch faces credibility test in OnlyFans era, expert says
item.description: Karol G and Cara Delevingne front Playboy's celebrity-driven relaunch as new editor Philip Picardi pivots to prestige storytelling.
item.content:encoded:
Playboy is back on the rack, now with a focus on "prestige" and storytelling alongside the nude images synonymous with the magazine. 

More than 70 years after Hugh Hefner founded the men's magazine brand, newly-appointed editor-in-chief Philip Picardi and David Miller, Playboy’s president of media and brand, were tasked with reviving the publication for an ever-changing audience.

A celebrity-focused relaunch to promote the iconic Playboy Playmates and Bunnies began in April with Colombian pop star Karol G on the cover. The sold-out print run lead efforts for a larger print run of its latest issue featuring supermodel Cara Delevingne in only a latex corset.

HOLLY MADISON SAYS PLAYBOY MANSION FELT LIKE A 'SAFE HARBOR' AFTER SHE ENCOUNTERED HOLLYWOOD'S DARK SIDE

Adrienne Uthe, founder of Kronus Communications, exclusively told Fox News Digital that it's essential for Playboy to evolve if they want to survive a reinvention.

"It's not about erasing who you were — it's about carrying the core of what made you iconic into a context that makes sense today," Uthe said. "Playboy knows what it is. The question is whether it can translate that into something the market is ready to receive."

CARA DELEVINGNE ISSUES STARK WARNING AFTER TERRIFYING DRUG ADDICTION

Picardi recently told the Press Gazette that focusing on what "initially drove its prestige and its buzz and its interest and its loyalty, which was of course its storytelling" was paramount for Playboy's revival.

"There’s always been a difference between Playboy and porn, especially the kinds of porn that are really prevalent today and that people have access to just by having a Twitter account or a Reddit account," Picardi told the outlet. "And so I actually found the challenge of bringing back an artfulness and a thoughtfulness to nudity to be super exciting as a creative."

In addition to featuring Playmates of the month, photoshoots are featured online and across social media platforms. Picardi also admitted that cover stars are "fully involved" in the creative process, including approval over photographers and stylists.

'BAYWATCH' STAR DONNA D'ERRICO SAYS PLAYBOY REJECTED HER 30 YEARS AFTER PROVOCATIVE COVER

"I stop short of calling what we do at Playboy feminist, because I think that’s a loaded term, and I’m sure many people would disagree with that qualification," Picardi said.

'BAYWATCH' STAR CARMEN ELECTRA SHARES WHAT MEN MUST AVOID WHEN PURSUING MIDDLE-AGED WOMEN

"But what I will say is what our approach with nudity has always been: that it is up to a woman how much she has to show on set … when we make sure that the permission lies with the woman herself, we’re always surprised that she feels very liberated to make the calls and do what she wants to do, which is often showing more than we expected or thought of."

Uthe agreed that power dynamics were still very much in play, albeit with unbalanced scales.

"The cultural headwinds are real — but they're not as uniformly strong as people assume," Uthe said. "Yes, #MeToo shifted the conversation around how women are viewed. But look at OnlyFans: over 300 million registered users, creators earning billions annually.

"The appetite for adult content and sexual expression didn't disappear — it just moved, and in many cases toward platforms where women hold the power."

PLAYMATE OF THE YEAR FOR PLAYBOY MAGAZINE COMEBACK SAYS ONLYFANS IS NO COMPETITION

She added, "That's actually Playboy's opening if they're smart enough to take it. The challenge isn't the market. The challenge is credibility. Can Playboy convince a generation that grew up with creator-controlled content that a legacy brand can authentically stand for the same thing?"

Celebrity partnerships may be "critical" at this stage in the game, but Uthe warned it only works if "the alignment is real."

"Karol G and Cara Delevingne aren't random choices, and that's the point," she said. "Every partnership is a positioning signal right now. The danger is chasing names instead of synonymy.

"The partners need to embody what Playboy is becoming, not just lend their audience to it. Done right, this accelerates everything. Done wrong, it reads as desperate."

FORMER PLAYBOY MODEL HOLLY MADISON ADMITS BEDROOM ACTIVITIES SHE DIDN'T LIKE TO DO WITH HUGH HEFNER

Despite heightened interest in publishing a new version of past pleasures, the brand still has an uphill battle.

"Playboy should show, not tell," Uthe said. "There's something worth saving — the cultural footprint, the iconography, the permission to talk about sexuality openly and unapologetically. But hope isn't a strategy."

"The brands that make it back are the ones that stop defending their legacy and start building the next chapter. Playboy has the name. The question is whether they have the discipline to survive."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

The once-monthly print magazine went quarterly in 2019 and then shuttered in March 2020 during the pandemic. Special print issues were released before CEO Ben Kohn brought in a new team.

In 2025, the company made $120.9 million, with a net loss of $12.7 million, compared to $79.4 million in 2024, according to an earnings release.

Miller said there's interest in reviving Playboy-style parties, sporting events and research, but that they still want to make the brand "respectful, and we want to make it modern, and feel like it’s inviting and inclusive for all individuals."

Media executive Derek Reisfield, who previously served as CBS's vice president of business development, noted that Hefner was "very creative" about his brand growth and distribution.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

"He had the Playboy clubs ... It was a lifestyle thing," Reisfeld told Fox News Digital. "They had licensing, which is almost pure profit. The magazine was this great engine that then also enabled them to get into filmed entertainment with the Playboy Channel."

He added, "But the Internet came along and basically blew all these guys out of the water because there was a proliferation of pornography and semi-pornography, and it was all accessible. It was pretty cheap and and so that sort of killed it.

"They're trying to figure out how do I thread the needle in this new world where all this stuff is available to people and a lot of it's not really differentiated."

Celebrities and earned media appear to be the driving force for the business, which Reisfield admitted was "difficult positioning" for the publication.

"You have to get it just right, and I don't know if they have," he said. "I would want to do very extensive research about who the customer is, what trade-offs they're making, what their current media habits are, where there's an opening and where the heritage of Playboy can be deployed.

He added, "It's got to be true to the brand or what people believe the brand is."

item.title: Urgent Yellowstone fishing closures hit 3 rivers as water turns deadly
item.description: Dead trout are reported in the Firehole River as water temperatures hit 86 degrees, prompting Yellowstone National Park to shut down three major rivers.
item.content:encoded:
Visitors heading to Yellowstone National Park this summer will find some of the park's best-known fishing rivers closed due to extreme water temperatures.

In a July 15 announcement, National Park Service (NPS) officials from Yellowstone said the Madison, Firehole and Gibbon rivers would be temporarily closed to fishing until further notice.

The affected rivers are all located on Yellowstone's west and northwest sides, near some of the park's busiest attractions, and the Firehole River flows through the Old Faithful area.

HIDDEN DANGER AT POPULAR US NATIONAL PARK FORCES TRAIL CLOSURES AS BEAR SIGHTINGS CLIMB

The closures are in effect 24 hours a day. Officials blamed unusually warm water temperatures, which can be lethal to Yellowstone's famed trout.

"In recent days, peak water temperatures have ranged from 80 to 86 degrees Fahrenheit during the day," the notice said.

"Coupled with high air temperatures, these conditions are lethal to trout, and there have been reports of dead fish in the Firehole River."

Officials added there are currently no heat-related fishing restrictions on other lakes, rivers or streams in the park.

CAPTAIN HAULS IN NEARLY 500-POUND SEA GIANT AFTER GRUELING BATTLE, FEEDS MOST OF TOWN

The temporary closures will "protect the park's native and wild trout fisheries and will remain in effect until conditions improve," according to the NPS.

Trout struggle to survive in exceptionally warm water because their bodies warm along with their surroundings, said Steven J. Cooke, a biology professor at Carleton University in Canada.

"Fish are ectotherms, which means that their body temperature is the same as the water temperature," Cooke told Fox News Digital. 

"At high temperatures, trout struggle to obtain enough oxygen and may not recover upon release."

ANGLER EXPECTING A FISH PULLS UP RARE 700-YEAR-OLD SWORD: ‘REAL TREASURES’

Cooke, based in Ottawa, Ontario, added that anglers "need to get used to such closures, as they will become more common." 

"However, if anglers up their game and improve their fish handling by minimizing fight time and eliminating air exposure, then that will be better for fish and allow continued fishing," he said.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"We are seeing the same thing play out with muskellunge anglers in the Midwest, or Atlantic salmon anglers in the Northeast."

Cooke, who is on the board of the nonprofit organization Keep Fish Wet, said regulations like fishing closures "make more sense" in national parks, to ensure sensitive resources are protected.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

Keep Fish Wet educates anglers on science-based best practices for catch-and-release fishing to maximize fish survival rates.

According to the NPS's website, Yellowstone has long been a popular destination for anglers.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS

"Yellowstone supports some of the world's most famous fisheries and has been a destination for generations of anglers for over 150 years," the site states.

"Fishing regulations are designed to maintain native fish abundance and genetic integrity, the park's overall ecological integrity, and a recreational fishery for park visitors."

Fox News Digital reached out to the NPS for comment.

item.title: Amazon Teacher of the Year winner accused of preying on 14-year-old student
item.description: Tymond Tran, a 2022 Amazon Future Engineer Teacher of the Year recipient, faces rape and indecent assault charges involving a 14-year-old student.
item.content:encoded:
A former Massachusetts middle school teacher who received a national teaching award from Amazon is accused of raping a 14-year-old student and committing other offenses during the 2022-23 school year, prosecutors said.

The Essex County District Attorney’s Office said 38-year-old Tymond Tran, of Hudson, New Hampshire, pleaded not guilty Wednesday in Essex County Superior Court to charges of rape of a child aggravated by an age difference of 10 years or more, indecent assault and battery on a person 14 or over, exhibiting a child in a nude or lascivious pose, and distributing obscene matter to a minor.

Prosecutors allege the offenses involved a then-14-year-old student while Tran taught eighth grade at Thurgood Marshall Middle School in Lynn during the 2022-23 school year.

CALIFORNIA TEACHER SAMANTHA WATSON ARRESTED FOR ALLEGED SEXUAL CONDUCT WITH STUDENT AT CHARTER SCHOOL

Tran remains free under strict court-ordered conditions while the case proceeds.

Judge John C. Fraser ordered Tran to remain on house arrest with GPS monitoring except for emergency medical appointments, court appearances and attorney visits.

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

Tran must also have no unsupervised contact with anyone under 18, submit to random searches of his electronic devices, reside at an Attleboro address, avoid all contact with victims and witnesses, and possess no child sexual abuse material.

TEACHER ACCUSED OF SEX WITH STUDENTS WAS ALLEGEDLY BLACKMAILED FOR BETTER GRADES: WARRANTS

He is scheduled to return to court Aug. 26 for a pretrial conference.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

Attorney Megan Stanley, who represents Tran, did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's request for comment.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE

In 2022, Tran was named one of 10 recipients of Amazon's Future Engineer Teacher of the Year Award. Amazon says the annual honor recognizes educators who promote diversity, equity and inclusion while encouraging students to pursue careers in computer science and robotics.

GOT A TIP?

The company said recipients were selected for inspiring students through innovative computer science instruction and for expanding access to STEM education in their schools and communities.

Fox News Digital has reached out to the Lynn School District and Amazon for comment on the matter.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Lynn officials confirmed to Boston.com that Tran worked in the city's public school district from 2021 to 2024 and left voluntarily before achieving professional status.

GET BREAKING NEWS BY EMAIL

"We are shocked and disturbed by this news," Lynn Mayor Jared C. Nicholson and Superintendent of Schools Molly Cohen said in a joint statement provided to the outlet. "Our primary concern is for the well-being of the former LPS student. Our hope for that student is healing and justice."

The officials said the district is cooperating with law enforcement, conducting an independent investigation and making support available to students, families and staff affected by the allegations.

item.title: Sophie Cunningham sends message to her haters after saying men don't belong in women's sports
item.description: Sophie Cunningham tripled down on Instagram after her ESPN comments about biological men in women's sports sparked backlash from sports media.
item.content:encoded:
Sophie Cunningham said what she said, libs. Quit complaining about it. Quit asking her about it. Quit crying over it.

The WNBA's biggest star right now told ESPN earlier this week that she didn't think men should be in women's sports (novel concept!), and she's not backing down.

First, she doubled down before Wednesday's game against the Sun, telling a giant group of reporters who grilled her for FOUR MINUTES on her comments that she "said what I said" and believed it was "kind of common sense."

SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM DOUBLES DOWN AHEAD OF SUN GAME AFTER ESPN PROFILE STIRS CONTROVERSY: 'I SAID WHAT I SAID'

Thursday, Cunningham took to her Instagram to reiterate her beliefs, sending a blunt message to the haters with a simple "I said what I said" and a saluting emoji.

What a patriot:

I've said it a billion times this season, and I'm now more sure than ever ...

Sophie Cunningham is the most marketable player in the WNBA right now. Not Caitlin Clark. Not Angel Reese. Not that one girl who we traded for from Russia.

Sophie Cunningham.

LEXIE HULL AND SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM TEAM UP ON A CALIFORNIA BEACH FOR SOME 'MUCH NEEDED R&R'

She's authentic. She's SANE. She's good at basketball. Yes, she's attractive. Marketing folks like that, too. But she's also fearless in what she says, and what she does.

That resonates so much with America right now, especially the sane part of the country that has to listen to the nonsense day in and day out.

Nonsense like all the lefty sports media outlets and journalists FREAKING out because Sophie Cunningham told ESPN men don't belong in women's locker rooms.

"I got a lot of negative feedback about me hating trans. And I'm like, 'I never once said that,'" Cunningham told the outlet. "I think that I am here to extend love. But I also think with that love is truth, being honest. And I want to protect young girls in a locker room, or young girls in sport who shouldn't have to go against biological men.

"I'm very much in the middle. I agree with things on both sides, disagree with things on both sides. And that's all I've ever said about my political beliefs. But people love to assume."

That's what folks are furious about right now. Seriously. That's all Sophie Cunningham said, and it let to a an absolute GRILLING before Wednesday's game.

Look at this. Look at these people:

My God. Just amazing. These people are truly amazing. I can't believe they exist in real life.

Anyway, Cunningham wasn't fazed, clearly. She took it all in stride, and has since doubled — and tripled — down on her comments.

What a breath of fresh air.

item.title: Raiders TE Brock Bowers impresses women with his hidden camera engagement video, Royals fan arrested & 510 lbs
item.description: Brock Bowers proposed to his girlfriend Cameron Newell in a hidden camera engagement video that has influencers gushing, but staging questions linger.
item.content:encoded:
Here we are for another Friday Screencaps & July just keeps chugging along with below-normal temps (for now) and an upcoming weekend tee time on my calendar for the first time this month. After the golf trip blowout in June, I had to lay low, which I accomplished. Now it's back to the links and celebrating summer, but, first, we have to analyze this Brock Bowers engagement video that was posted on Thursday.

Bowers is the Raiders tight end who will likely end up in the Pro Football Hall of Fame. At some point this week, he proposed to his influencer girlfriend, and Tennessee grad, Cameron Newell. The key here is that this is being sold as a hidden camera proposal, with perfect audio, and blue checkmark Instagram influencers are eating it up.

"IM SOBBING YOU GUYSSSS," Kenny Pickett's wife gushed on Instagram.

I'm not. I'm wondering how the production team pulled this off. Are they mic'd up? Did some wedding planner plant microphones in the ornamental grasses?

And before your wife attacks me with hate emails, I'm truly happy for these two. Brock's 23. He could've gone into next week's training camp without popping the question. He could've put his head down and grinded through another NFL season with a loyal girlfriend, but the guy stepped up for humanity.

Now they'll probably have like four kids and repopulate the U.S. I'm going to celebrate that every single time.

TAYLOR SWIFT AND TRAVIS KELCE'S WEDDING COULD UNLEASH UNPRECEDENTED GLOBAL SPENDING FRENZY: EXPERT

I just can't get over these modern engagements where the woman acts like she's completely shocked. What am I to believe anymore? Do people actually read books on the beach? Are NFL tight end engagement videos staged? Was Taylor Swift in on decorating her engagement stage where Travis popped the question?

And while we're at it, are those card breaks where the influencers find some 1/1 card worth $500k staged?

My brain spends half of its day trying to figure out how it's being manipulated.

CRUISE SHIP HIT WITH THIRD STOMACH ILLNESS OUTBREAK IN TWO MONTHS, REVIEW UNDERWAY

📩 Email: joe.kinsey@outkick.com Send photos, stories, tips, rants—whatever you've got.

📰 Screencaps Page: 👉 Read the latest Screencaps

▶️ YouTube: Screencaps with Joe Kinsey Subscribe for videos, rants, and behind-the-scenes.

🐦 Twitter/X: @JoeKinseyexp Tag me or drop a DM.

📸 Instagram: @OutKickScreencaps You guys need to start tagging me on content you're seeing.

📘 Facebook Page: Screencaps on Facebook

👥 Facebook Group: Join the Screencaps Community

📬 Mail (Thursday Night Mowing League): 27072 Carronade Dr, Unit A 155 Perrysburg, OH 43551

🗞️ Newsletter: 👉 Subscribe here

Would it be weird to bring in industrial fans and shoot the smoke back at the neighbor? Last night, I was on the patio with Mrs. Screencaps. She was running drip tube to her plants. I was pulling weeds and watching/listening to the final 50 miles of stage 18 of the Tour de France when it happened.

It's happened multiple times over the years. The neighbor lady will light up a cig on a calm, cool night, and without fail, the cig smoke gets up into the jet stream and guess who gets to smell it? We do.

Guess who cannot have their windows open because the smoke blows right into our windows? Yep, us.

I was hoping to not have to bring in box fans to cleanse the air on our patio, but that might be my only option here. Save the emails telling me to have a conversation with the neighbor. No thanks.

Would it be weird to bring in a fan like they have on ski slopes?

Looking back on my career, there have been a few moments where I cannot believe I was receiving emails that turned into worldwide stories that will be remembered for decades and written into obituaries down the road.

Rex Ryan's foot fetish era was one of those moments I'll never forget. It started with a simple email to myself and a Deadspin writer from a guy with a foot fetish. The rest is history.

– Anonymous (he's written multiple times over the years, but I have to protect people in case The Masters is reading) emails: Joe, writing to see if Screencaps or the TNML can offer some help……I had some luck this week as my best friend got drawn for Wednesday tickets for the Masters. He very kindly gave me two of them to use (repaying the same favor I did for him 10 years ago)….the issue Is I have 2 daughters (20 and 18) who both want to go….so I am in need of finding a third ticket.  Can the community offer any advice on the best way to get a Wednesday ticket?

Kinsey: It's a great question that I believe will have a couple of simple answers: try buying on the secondary market and pray you don't get caught; or make the tough decision on which daughter gets to go and which one doesn't.

Or...dad gives up the two tickets to his daughters to go walk the grounds.

Talk about a crossroads moment in Anonymous' life. Let's face it, one of his daughters might not go, and may NEVER attend the Masters based on how hard it is to get tickets. Think of the immense pressure he's under.

Now, I will say that the emailer is a CONFIRMED Great American®, if you catch my drift. Some of you like to give back to those who have sacrificed for this country.

Needless to say, I'm intrigued how this plays out.

– Eric P. shares: I can tell you what explosive is in the context you mentioned by an experience in a Cabo shower when I thought it was a little squeaker but turned out to be new style of stucco that nobody needs on the walls..Yikes that took a lot of towels and a big tip for the cleaning woman.

– Jeff M. checks in: There is a whole generation that does not understand the lifelong brilliance of William Shatner. Nothing he does could surprise me at this point.

Take on multiple iconic pop culture roles? Got it.

Beat stage 4 cancer? No problem!

Record some unbelievable cover versions of classic rock songs? Why not!

Rocket Man: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BdUMICxLXhM Lucy in the Sky: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AB3uVARNhmMTambourine Man: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_0hTtsqiFCc

– John C. in Bowling Green, KY has something to get off his chest: Regarding the Cracker Barrel conversation, their problems are self-inflicted. I met with a men's group every week for breakfast there. The Old Timer's breakfast was $8.99 a short time ago. It's now $11.99, a 33% increase, but it now does not include grits and there is a $1 upcharge to substitute regular hash browns for the hash brown casserole. Even worse is that a cup of regular coffee is $3.39. Shameful!

Between stores selling Halloween decorations in early July, to pumpkin spice creamer being bought by our Florida readers in the middle of July, it's time for me to reignite my RESPECT SUMMER mantra.

###################

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

I want you guys to go out and show me this weekend how you're RESPECTING SUMMER and then I want you to send in that content. Please tag your emails with "Respecting Summer" in the title.

We cannot let the Pumpkin Spice Latte mafia destroy what we hold so dear. They can wait on hoodie weather. We're here to celebrate SUMMER.

Go enjoy it.

item.title: Iowa gubernatorial hopeful Zach Lahn tells 'Ruthless' Democrats are hiding their bad policies
item.description: Republican Iowa gubernatorial candidate Zach Lahn exposed Democratic candidate Rob Sand over his endorsements from liberal figures, such as Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez.
item.content:encoded:
Republican Iowa gubernatorial candidate Zach Lahn said Democratic opponent Rob Sand is pretending to be someone else during an exclusive interview on the "Ruthless Podcast."

"[The Democrats] are realizing that their ideas don't sell," Lahn said in an interview released Friday morning. "So what do they have to do? They have to pretend to be somebody they're not."

Lahn drew attention to Sand’s endorsements from national progressive figures like Sen. Elizabeth Warren, D-Mass., Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., and Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz.



A farmer and businessman, he noted that his campaign highlighted agricultural issues in a manner that resonates with voters on the ground.

ALASKA’S DAN SULLIVAN TELLS 'RUTHLESS' HE WILL OVERCOME DEM EFFORT TO ‘STEAL’ HIS NAME

"A lot of our campaign, even in the primary, people said, ‘you can't run a Republican primary running on the issues you're running on,’" said Lahn, who received the first endorsement from Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.'s Make America Healthy Again (MAHA) PAC. "And I said, ‘We can because this is what the people actually care about.' I know because I'm with them. I'm one of them."

Lahn shocked the political world when he won a crowded primary in early June, which included knocking off favorite Rep. Randy Feenstra, R-Iowa, who was endorsed by President Donald Trump. Trump has since endorsed Lahn.

Sand's campaign fired back in a comment to Fox News Digital.

"Zach Lahn is a career political operative who can’t answer basic questions about where he lives," Sand deputy campaign manager Emma O'Brien said. "Meanwhile, Rob Sand has support from across the state and across the aisle, including from over 2,400 registered Republicans and over 6,000 no-party voters, because Iowans trust him from his work as State Auditor and know him from his regular visits to their communities. Iowans aren’t buying what career political operative Zach Lahn is trying to sell them."

Podcast co-host Josh Holmes commended Lahn for his unique approach to healthcare policy.

"You don't get a lot of Republicans nationwide that want to weigh deeply into it," Holmes said.

CLICK HERE TO FOLLOW THE RUTHLESS PODCAST

Earlier this week, Fox News released its projections for gubernatorial races across the country. In Iowa, the Democrats are currently favored to beat Lahn. However, no Democrat has won Iowa's governor's race since 2006, and the state has also gone for Trump handily in three straight presidential elections.

Holmes had an optimistic tone when analyzing the race.

"What has happened to the Democratic Party over the last 20 years, in particular in states like yours, is that they just no longer resonate," Holmes said. "They’re culturally divorced from what it is that families are dealing with on a day-to-day basis."

The interview took place as part of the "Ruthless" Midterm Interview Series, an ongoing initiative to interview major candidates across the country. The hosts have already interviewed candidates in 18 states, with more scheduled ahead of the November midterms.

‘FINISH THE DAMN JOB’: HAGERTY TELLS 'RUTHLESS PODCAST' THAT TRUMP WILL COMPLETE IRAN MISSION

FOLLOW FOX NEWS FLASH ON FACEBOOK

"I don't want to go anywhere else but this seat to be the governor," Lahn said, comparing his aspirations to Sand’s. "I want to help make Iowa the best place to raise a family, start businesses, all of these things. And I think we've got a good foundation right now. But, you know, this is what I want to do. The New York Times has my opponent, I think, two days ago, as a very likely person to jump right into a presidential campaign."

item.title: Secret Service offers glimpse into WHCA Dinner security after alleged Trump assassination attempt
item.description: The Secret Service reveals enhanced security for the rescheduled WHCA Dinner at the Waldorf Astoria, including magnetometer screening and closures.
item.content:encoded:
The Secret Service is giving a behind-the-scenes glimpse inside the strategy to protect President Donald Trump and guests at Friday night's rescheduled White House Correspondents' Association (WHCA) Dinner.

The originally scheduled event in April was done before it even started after a crazed gunman bolted through a security checkpoint and was later accused of trying to assassinate the 47th president.

"Our agency grows and adapts from every event we secure, and we have engaged with WHCA to ensure attendees are given timely information in the unlikely event on an emergency during the event," Anthony Guglielmi, Secret Service's communications director, told Fox News Digital.

Guglielmi noted that the Secret Service's protection plan worked during the April 25 dinner when agents stopped Cole Allen of California after he allegedly descended from the 10th floor of the Washington Hilton Hotel, where the dinner is historically held, and attempted to breach security barriers. He allegedly fired one shot from a shotgun before tripping and falling, after which he was quickly detained. One Secret Service agent was shot and sustained minor injuries.

FIRST ON FOX: WHITE HOUSE REVEALS WHO’S JOINING TRUMP AT CORRESPONDENTS’ DINNER — AND WHO CAN’T ATTEND

The rescheduled dinner is set for Friday night at the Waldorf Astoria on Pennsylvania Avenue, which is housed inside the Old Post Office Tower and was previously owned by the president and called the Trump International Hotel. It's the first time in decades that the annual WHCA Dinner is not being held at the Washington Hilton.

Those attending the dinner will enter the ballroom through a door separate from the main lobby and entrance of the hotel.

"Guests will see a visible police presence around the Waldorf Astoria hotel and there will be enhanced traffic closures due to proximity to major thoroughfares," Guglielmi said. "All ticketed guests will go through airport-style magnetometer screening to enter the event."

"Roads immediately around the hotel will be closed Friday evening, and specifics about closures will be communicated in the coming days."

TRUMP URGES NEED FOR SECURE WHITE HOUSE BALLROOM AFTER DINNER SHOOTING, SAYS HOTEL SETTING ‘TOUGH’ TO PROTECT

Guglielmi added that exact arrival instructions will be communicated specifically to ticket holders, and that each ticket will be screened by event staff prior to Secret Service security.

Notably, the dinner is significantly scaled-back from the usual event, which typically hosts 4,000+ guests inside one of the largest ballrooms in the District of Columbia — second only to the convention center Marriott Marquis. The Waldorf Astoria ballroom can host a maximum of 1,300 people for tight banquet seating.

At the dinner, Secret Service Police Officer Victor Gonzales, who was shot by the alleged gunman, will be awarded the President’s Award for Exceptional Service to the WHCA for his actions protecting Trump and event guests during the April attack.

"The WHCA is deeply grateful that law enforcement officials prevented the armed suspect from entering the ballroom," said Weijia Jiang of CBS News, who as president of the association presided over the April dinner.

"Officer Gonzales played a significant role when he ran toward danger so that thousands of others could make it home safely. His courage stemmed from a dedication to service, which we are looking forward to honoring this week."

WHCD SHOOTING SUSPECT PLANNED TO TARGET TRUMP OFFICIALS, MANIFESTO REVEALS

According to a court filing in the proceedings against Allen, the alleged gunman boarded an Amtrak train on April 21 after purchasing a one-way ticket from Los Angeles to the nation's capital, stopping in Chicago to change trains. While he rode, prosecutors say he "kept a running note on his phone of his observations and thoughts during his cross-country train journey."

His musings along the way, in tandem with what he wrote in a later manifesto, paint a picture of an unfocused person whose thoughts were "scattered," as one former FBI behavioral analyst said, despite the gravity of the situation.

While he traveled through the U.S. Southwest on the first leg of his trip, Allen made a note: "[t]he southwest desert in spring Distant wind turbines looming like snowy mountains across the hazy NM desert."

Of Chicago, where he would switch trains and board a second train to his final destination, Allen wrote that, "Chicago is cool; kinda like an Iowa small town was scaled up to LA size." Of the sliver of southwestern Pennsylvania through which he would pass, he wrote that the "woods are awesome (look like vast fairy lands filled with tiny trickling creeks in spring apparently."

Allen arrived in Washington, D.C., early in the afternoon on Friday, April 24. He spent about 30 hours in the city before initiating his alleged attack.

Surveillance video from the Washington Hilton hotel, also released by the Department of Justice, showed Allen apparently pacing through hallways, once entering the hotel's fitness center and taking a look around before hastily exiting.

Minutes before the attack, a pre-scheduled email from Allen was sent to his family and friends, explaining his actions, according to authorities.

He allegedly acknowledged that his mission would likely severely harm him at the least, but never stated that he was willing to die for his cause. His motivations were political, and he painted himself as a savior of the oppressed. He apologized profusely to family, friends and everyone he had come in contact with on his cross-country trek. He noted that there were certain people he hoped wouldn't be caught in the crossfire, and described himself as "friendly."

Allen remains in federal custody in Washington, D.C., while awaiting trial on four charges.

Those charges are attempting to assassinate the President of the United States, discharge of a firearm during a crime of violence, transporting a firearm across state lines and assaulting a federal officer with a deadly weapon.

Fox News' Jake Gibson contributed to this report.

item.title: 'Ransom Canyon' star Josh Duhamel says he'd rather put money into North Dakota after selling Los Angeles home
item.description: Josh Duhamel splits time between Fargo and Los Angeles while raising his family off the grid, revealing how ranch life informs his Netflix role.
item.content:encoded:
Josh Duhamel is ready to set down roots somewhere other than Los Angeles.

In a recent interview with Fox News Digital, the 53-year-old actor discussed season two of "Ransom Canyon," as well as his decision to sell his LA home following his move to North Dakota.

"Well, we still have a house in Los Angeles that we have because I have a 12-year-old son who goes to school there and will be there until he's done with school," he said. "So we go back and forth between Fargo, North Dakota and Los Angeles. The house that I just sold was the house that was sort of my transition space between when I got divorced and when I started a new family."

He explained that after "renting it for the last couple of years," he decided to sell it, adding, he realized he would "rather put that money in North Dakota."

‘SOPRANOS’ STAR JAMIE-LYNN SIGLER FLED HOLLYWOOD FOR TEXAS WHERE SHE NO LONGER FEELS ‘WATCHED’

The "Life as We Know It" star sold the 3,310-square-foot $2.9 million home in Encino earlier this month, a little over a year after he spoke to Parade about his decision to move out of Los Angeles to spend time in Minnesota, living an off-the-grid lifestyle in a home he spent 15 years building.

"The closest store is 40 miles away," he explained to the outlet. "Once we get there, it's really about everybody taking care of each other — making memories, spending time with family and friends."

WATCH: Josh Duhamel would rather invest his money in North Dakota

Duhamel lives in Fargo, North Dakota, with his wife, Audra Mari, and their two kids – son Shepard, 2, and daughter Rocca, who is 2 months old – with his 12-year-old son, Axl, whom he shares with his ex Fergie, joining them from time to time. The family will often spend time at their off-the-grid cabin in Minnesota.

In addition to allowing his family to "get back to the basics," and his children to grow up exploring nature rather than being on their iPads all day, Duhamel says living an off-the-grid lifestyle in their Minnesota getaway has helped him prepare for his role in "Ransom Canyon."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

"I think that when I first met with April Blair, the showrunner, I was at my cabin, literally out there on my tractor doing something, and I was kind of in that headspace already," he said. "There are a lot of similarities. I don't have a horse, I don't have cattle, but I do have a lot of tractors, a lot of ranchy stuff out there, and I think that I sort of live this life, or something closely adjacent to it."

He added: "Anyway, so I felt like I really sort of understood the love of your land, the legacy, taking care of the people around you, all those things that I think are sort of the same themes as ranch life. And I think that that, they, do sort of inform one another."

The show is a neo-Western drama on Netflix starring Duhamel and Minka Kelly, and follows the lives of three rival ranching families in the Texas Hill Country.

WATCH: Josh Duhamel's off-the-grid lifestyle helped prepare him for his 'Ransom Canyon' role

In the show, Duhamel plays the owner of Double K Ranch, one of the biggest ranches in the canyon, who in season one is still grieving the loss of his wife and son, and is willing to do anything to protect his family's land and legacy.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Season one of the show saw a budding romance between Duhamel and Kelly's characters, who plays his wife's best friend who has always had feelings for him.

"Constant sort of balance that we're trying to strike on the show in that I want to make sure that we keep it rough and sort of gritty and cowboy Western and then there's forces that want to pull it way into the romance, which I get," he said. "But it's finding that balance between the two so it's still a Western, and it still has that kind of gritty Western vibe."

"Ransom Canyon" season 2 is now streaming on Netflix.

item.title: Connecticut Sun's Saniya Rivers melts down on social media over Caitlin Clark's scoreboard taunt
item.description: Saniya Rivers fired back on Threads after Caitlin Clark pointed to the scoreboard during a 35-point blowout, insisting she'll never tolerate disrespect.
item.content:encoded:
Connecticut Sun guard Saniya Rivers wasn't taking Wednesday night's loss quietly after Caitlin Clark and the Indiana Fever steamrolled the team 123-88.

After taking the loss and the taunting, Rivers took to Threads to defend her pride and insist she had zero regrets about jawing with Clark: "Girl idgaf if we're down by 50, I'm NEVER tolerating disrespect. As long as there's time left on the clock, ima keep hoopin. period."

The whole dustup boiled down to one question: Did Clark pointing to the scoreboard during a 35-point blowout somehow cross the line?

CAITLIN CLARK PROVES THERE'S NO NBA 2K27 COVER CURSE AS FEVER SET FRANCHISE SCORING RECORD

With 28.8 seconds left in the third quarter and Indiana cruising 95-67, Rivers got in Clark's face after a routine foul and told her she was getting "locked up."

Meanwhile, Clark was busy hanging 27 points and 11 assists on the Sun on 8-of-13 shooting in just 27 minutes.

Clark didn't waste time arguing. She repeatedly pointed to the scoreboard in front of Rivers and barked, "Scoreboard!"

NBA'S ADAM SILVER PUSHED WNBA COMMISSIONER TO SUSPEND ALYSSA THOMAS FOR CAITLIN CLARK THROAT JAB: REPORT

Officials quickly stepped in anyway, issuing double technical fouls. The whistle did carry real consequences for Indiana, giving Clark her seventh technical of the season. Under WNBA rules, an eighth technical triggers an automatic one-game suspension without pay, leaving the league's biggest draw one whistle away from sitting out.

It has been another eventful week for Clark, who continues to rack up rivals on the court and critics off it. Rivers is simply the latest name added to that growing list.

USA Today columnist Nancy Armour recently drew widespread backlash after invoking Emmett Till in an opinion piece criticizing Clark and her fan base, another example of how even routine moments involving the Fever star quickly snowball into national talking points.

While critics hyperventilate and opponents lecture about respect, Clark keeps selling out arenas, drawing record television audiences and giving people a reason to watch the WNBA.

Send us your thoughts: alejandro.avila@outkick.com / Follow along on X: @alejandroaveela

item.title: Boasberg delivers fresh immigration setback to Trump in free-speech clash
item.description: Judge James Boasberg found the Trump administration's visa policy may constitute viewpoint discrimination by targeting those favoring content moderation.
item.content:encoded:
Chief U.S. District Judge James Boasberg has temporarily halted the Trump administration’s effort to deny visas to individuals it claims worked to perpetuate censorship against Americans.

Boasberg, who has been a major judicial antagonist across President Donald Trump's two administrations, handed down a decision earlier this month blocking the State Department from restricting visas for foreign officials and other foreign nationals considered responsible for or complicit in suppressing American free speech. He found that, while the federal government has a "legitimate interest in responding when foreign officials use sovereign power to suppress protected expression in the United States," it is possible that the government is engaging in viewpoint discrimination, and thus violating the First Amendment, through its policy.

"The policy, at its core, does not burden all speech about platforms, all research into content moderation, or all advocacy about online harms," Boasberg wrote. "It presses its enforcement thumb against one side of the scale: the view that platforms should do more to moderate content, label disinformation, restrict abuse, share data with researchers, or take responsibility for the harms their systems amplify."

GOP LAWMAKERS URGE SUPREME COURT TO STRENGTHEN FIRST AMENDMENT PROTECTIONS FOR STREET PREACHERS

"The Government, in other words, has not set itself against everyone who speaks about platform governance. It has set itself against those whose work favors more moderation rather than less," he added.

The State Department’s policy followed a Biden-era controversy where the Global Disinformation Index (GDI), which produced alleged "blacklists" of certain news websites, received funding from the federal government. Conservatives have argued that GDI’s ratings contributed to advertisers withholding revenue from conservative outlets and that federal support for the organization implicated free-speech concerns.

The State Department’s policy paused by Boasberg restricted visa access to individuals involved with GDI and similar organizations.

"The Trump Administration believes that aliens who are or were involved or complicit in censoring American citizens must face appropriate consequences," a spokesperson for the department told Fox News Digital. "An American visa is a privilege, not a right. Specific questions about this lawsuit should be referred to the Department of Justice."  

DC APPEALS COURT ORDERS JUDGE BOASBERG TO HALT TRUMP CONTEMPT PROBE OVER DEPORTATION FLIGHTS

Boasberg has presided over several high-profile cases challenging actions by Trump’s second administration. He temporarily blocked the administration’s rapid deportation of Venezuelan migrants and ordered officials to preserve Signal messages in which senior national security officials discussed planned U.S. strikes in Yemen.

The clashes have made Boasberg a target of Trump, who called for his impeachment.

The Justice Department filed a misconduct complaint against him and accused him of overreach. Chief Justice John Roberts rejected impeachment as a response to an unfavorable ruling.

Boasberg was first nominated to the federal bench by President Barack Obama in 2010.

JUDGE ORDERS ICE TO FREE WISCONSIN MOSQUE LEADER OVER 'SUBSTANTIAL' FREE SPEECH CLAIM AFTER CRITICIZING ISRAEL

"A visa is a privilege—not a constitutional entitlement," Sen. Eric Schmitt, R-Mo., wrote of the decision on X. "The First Amendment protects the American people’s right to speak. It does not require the American people to admit foreign actors who work to restrict that speech."

The case against the State Department’s policy was initially brought by the Coalition for Independent Technology Research (CITR), a nonprofit organization representing academics and journalists.

While Boasberg issued a stay temporarily preventing the State Department from enforcing its visa policy, he has not yet ruled on the merits of the case, meaning that the policy could still survive his court. Additionally, the State Department could still seek appellate review of his interim order blocking their policy.

CITR and the State Department did not respond to requests for comment after being reached by Fox News Digital on Thursday.

item.title: Running out of time: SEC, Big Ten wait on 'Protect College Sports Act' revisions as August recess nears
