item.description: SEC commissioner Greg Sankey warns that delays past August recess could doom the Protect College Sports Act as Congress shifts to other priorities.
item.content:encoded:
TAMPA, FL - While the SEC was holding its annual kickoff to the upcoming football season this week in Florida, the bigger conversation was transpiring in Washington, D.C., around the 'Protect College Sports Act'.

After being passed through the Senate Commerce Committee in June, Sens. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) and Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.) are racing against time for the legislation aimed at creating guardrails around NIL, eligibility issues, transfers and even a conference realignment, with the SEC and Big Ten not providing their support for the bill in its current form.

CLOCK IS TICKING AS SENATORS TED CRUZ, MARIA CANTWELL PUSH FOR SEC, BIG TEN BUY-IN ON COLLEGE SPORTS BILL

The bipartisan bill was largely created to help navigate college athletics back into a realistic situation, and out of courtrooms. After years of lobbying Congress for help, which led to the SCORE Act being created before this latest bill, there is a growing dissension between parties on how much actual help is wanted at the federal level.

After meeting numerous times this week, the revisions currently being discussed have not been presented to either conference as of Thursday evening.

Obviously, time is of the essence, which Senate Majority Leader John Thune referenced to reporters on Thursday afternoon, with him mentioning that it doesn’t look as though it will reach the Senate floor before Congress goes on its August recess.

According to sources close to the situation in Washington, Senators Ted Cruz and Maria Cantwell are still working to get the bill to the Senate floor before the recess, while also continuing to work out revisions with the SEC and Big Ten regarding the legislation.

Also, there has been pushback on the thought that the bill is dead with the news that a vote would likely not take place in August.

"They're working on language through the night, and that's probably not how they'd be spending their Thursday night if there wasn't a chance," Sen. Maria Cantwell spokesperson Ansley Lacitis told Fox News/OutKick

GREG SANKEY ADDRESSES SEC BREAKAWAY TALK AS MICHIGAN TRIES TO STEAL SPOTLIGHT WITH AD WARDE MANUEL DECISION

Although the SEC and Big Ten conferences have repeatedly asked for congressional help, both feel as though the current bill is unfairly putting them in a corner, especially if the two are not allowed to add additional schools in the future.

In addition to previously reported issues, the section regarding the pooling of media rights has left a bad taste in the mouth of conference leaders from the SEC and Big Ten, with some voicing their displeasure that this current form would lead to them losing money on future television rights deals.

Senate advances landmark Protect College Sports Act, ignoring SEC and Big Ten opposition with clock ticking

During an interview with OutKick on Wednesday afternoon in Tampa, where he was asked how real the August timeline is, SEC commissioner Greg Sankey pointed out that if this were to drag on until Congress returns, there would be more important issues to be discussed in the House and Senate.

"I consider it a milestone (August timeline), the August recess," Sankey noted. "Congress does return in September. Then once they’re through in September, you have budget battles, we've seen how those play out. So, it is important to try and keep moving, that has been a part of our communication, and there has been a lot of energy and effort expended by Senators, which we appreciate, to try and make adjustments.

"I think the reality is, if a bill is produced and advances, and it's eventually signed into law, the view will be we've solved those problems. And, Congress is going to move onto taxation, funding whatever, international issues. You just make a list, they have a lot of important issues, and they’re not coming back to close gaps or solve problems. That's why since the act was introduced, we've been focused on common sense adjustments to close the loopholes and make this manageable. There's energy and effort in that, but that doesn’t mean we're at the finish line yet."

If both felt as though the pooling of media rights was actually 'voluntary', which both Cruz and Cantwell have said is the case, then there would most likely be further support from conference members.

But, at the moment, the SEC and Big Ten are waiting to see if their input on changes to the bill would actually come to fruition, with revisions that neither side have seen as of Thursday evening, as previously reported.

There is no doubt in the eyes of conference officials and congressional leaders in Washington that work is being done to get this bill to the Senate floor.

The bigger issue was how senators were going to get such a complicated matter condensed into a bill that spans 100 pages, with issues that are running rampant across college athletics.

But, there was always going to be a problem with timing.

Also, when you add in the potential limiting of conference expansion and revenue-sharing, there was going to be roadblocks.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

While work continues on trying to get this legislation onto the Senate floor for a vote, with the House being a whole separate issue as midterms loom, even if the revisions are presented to congressional leaders, along with the SEC and Big Ten, it does not mean we are getting a signed bill.

But, by the time this makes the Senate floor, whenever that is, there will most likely be another lawsuit filed pertaining to a player's eligibility.

Or, as we like to call it in the college athletics world, 'Just a day that ends in Y'.

item.title: Good Samaritan in Spider-Man costume helps man in wheelchair cross busy intersection in viral moment
item.description: Video from Jonesboro, Arkansas, traffic cameras captured a driver in a Spider-Man costume helping a person in a wheelchair cross an intersection.
item.content:encoded:
A good Samaritan wearing a Spider-Man costume was captured on video leaping out of his vehicle to help a man in a wheelchair cross a busy intersection in Arkansas in a moment that has gone viral.

The heartwarming scene was filmed by traffic cameras on Tuesday after Christopher Hellenthal, 20, said he clocked out of work at a trampoline park in Jonesboro.

"That morning we had a superhero day, and so I brought it for the toddlers, and they had a blast," Hellenthal said about the Spider-Man costume. "And whenever I got off work and clocked out, I was actually just going to head into town and just kind of drive around with the suit. And I noticed that there was this man, and he was in a wheelchair, and he was having a lot of trouble, he wasn’t going to make it."

"But I just got out real fast, and I just helped him along, adrenaline pumping through me. I just told the man, I said, ‘don’t worry I got you,’ and I guess he understood, and I got him to a safe spot, and I made it back to my vehicle and just drove off," he added.

CRUISE PASSENGER HAILED AS HERO AFTER JUMPING INTO WATER TO SAVE 84-YEAR-OLD MAN

"I would have done it with or without the suit," Hellenthal also said.

The moment was shared on the Facebook page of the Jonesboro Police Department, which called Hellenthal a "true superhero."

"We were able to spot this little web of kindness on our intersection cameras. Thank you, sir, for helping this gentleman reach his destination safely," police said.

Sally Smith, a police department spokesperson, told The Associated Press that "Not in my 7 years in the department have I witnessed this."

'PRO-LIFE SPIDERMAN' SENTENCED TO PRISON FOR CLIMBING LAS VEGAS SPHERE

Hellenthal, who lives in Lake City, east of Jonesboro, said he's always been a Spider-Man fan. He told the AP that he even had the opportunity to meet the late Stan Lee, one of the character’s co-creators.

Packy Magel, a manager at Ultimate Air Trampoline Park who works with Hellenthal, said he recognized Hellenthal from the video despite his superhero getup.

It’s "very much like him to do something like that and very much like him to be still in costume," Magel said to the AP.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

item.title: Blanche urged to investigate alleged foreign influence in fight against data centers
item.description: GOP lawmakers claim foreign-linked organizations tied to China and Russia are working to undermine U.S. data centers and AI infrastructure development.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: Lawmakers are calling on acting Attorney General Todd Blanche to investigate alleged foreign influence in the nationwide campaign to block construction of data centers.

A letter penned by Rep. Lance Gooden, R-Texas, and signed by multiple GOP lawmakers claims foreign-linked organizations and advocacy networks are trying to block the centers to give Chinq a leg up in the artificial intelligence (AI) race.

"We believe that the Chinese and also the Russians are colluding and working to make sure that the United States of America is not the leader when it comes to AI policy and development," Gooden told Fox News Digital in an interview. "They are so sophisticated with respect to disinformation and manipulating our processes here in the United States against us."

MARXIST ORGANIZER LEADS CAMPAIGN AGAINST VIRGINIA AI DATA CENTER AS FOREIGN INFLUENCE CONCERNS MOUNT

"We know historically, that they have funded [nongovernmental organizations] and nonprofits that don't disclose donations," Gooden added. "And then, they use these foreign funds against us and against our policy in a way that benefits them."

As AI demand accelerates, data centers have become essential to providing the computing power, electricity and infrastructure needed to process information and store massive amounts of data. However, proposed facilities often have faced opposition from critics who say they drive up utility costs and threaten the environment.

This past January, a self-described "organizer" for the Party for Socialism and Liberation (PSL), Jack Tensley, testified before the Frederick County Planning Commission in Maryland against the construction of a data center in Virginia.

TOP ENERGY GROUP WARNS WEALTHY FOREIGNERS ARE POTENTIALLY BANKROLLING ANTI-DATA CENTER CAMPAIGNS ACROSS US

"My really big fear here is the security risk. We have infrastructure attacks and failures, cyberattacks that happen — I want to know if this puts a target on the back of Frederick County," Tensley said. "I see a lot of risk here."

Tensley did not respond to Fox News Digital’s request for comment.

In a social media post from Party for Socialism and Liberation Boston, the organization openly opposed data centers, saying the control of AI and data centers is in the hands of billionaires.

HOUSE PANEL SUBPOENAS 3 LEFT-WING GROUPS IN PROBE OF $39M TIED TO MARXIST TYCOON NEVILLE SINGHAM

"Workers are rising up across the country in a growing movement against the tech billionaires – from fights against data centers in hundreds of cities, to labor organizing at the world’s biggest tech corporations," the post read.

Neville Roy Singham, a 72-year-old American who sold his tech company for $785 million in 2017 and now lives in Shanghai, is known to fund pro-Chinese Communist Party groups that operate in the U.S. He has funneled $278 million into the broad network of nonprofits since 2017, according to a Fox News Digital investigation.

Singham now faces a grand jury investigation over his financial activity in the Southern District of New York, a probe that Blanche authorized. Fox News Digital has reached out to Singham, who has not been charged with any crime to date, repeatedly for several months but has not received any responses.

The letter to Blanche cited reports from both the Bitcoin Policy Institute and OpenAI, both of which outlined the alleged role of foreign influence on campaigns against data center production.

OpenAI said that "two clusters of ChatGPT accounts likely originating from China" were "banned after they used our models in support of apparent covert influence operations that promoted narratives in an attempt to manipulate a legitimate debate about American AI and wider tech policies."

The Bitcoin Policy Institute found that PSL has organized anti-data center campaigns in at least 14 states, opposing roughly $23.6 billion in AI investments.

"Recent reports have also identified involvement of China-based tycoon Neville Roy Singham and his network of Marxist, communist, and socialist nonprofit organizations in influencing American AI discourse," the letter to Blanche read. "A BPI report from last month also uncovered specific instances of a nationwide influence campaign to undermine the American AI supply chain being led by the Party for Socialism and Liberation (PSL), an unincorporated political party that acts as 'the political arm of the Singham network' with ties to socialist governments like Cuba and Venezuela."

Fox News Digital asked the Justice Department if it was planning to respond to lawmakers or open an investigation but did not receive a response.

item.title: Mystery of gold coin shipwreck finally unmasked after 30 years: 'Fantastic conclusion'
item.description: More than 400 Moroccan gold coins helped researchers identify the Dom van Keulen, a Dutch trading ship that sank off Devon's coast back in 1633. Here are the details.
item.content:encoded:
Researchers in the United Kingdom believe they've finally identified a shipwreck off England's southern coast — a discovery three decades in the making.

The identification was announced in June by Bournemouth University and centers on a shipwreck discovered near Salcombe, Devon, in 1995.

At the time, divers recovered more than 400 Moroccan gold coins — but there was no clue as to what the ship was called and where it came from.

1,740-YEAR-OLD ROMAN COIN BEARING EMPEROR'S NAME FOUND AS ENGLAND RAIL DIG REVEALS CLUES

Now, researchers have identified it as the Dutch trading ship Dom van Keulen, a vessel that left Morocco for the Netherlands in the fall of 1633.

At the time, the ship "sprang a leak and sank," according to the university's news release. All crew members survived.

The findings are detailed in the recently published book "From Morocco to the Coast of England: The Story of the Dom van Keulen and its Remarkable Cargo."

SHAKESPEARE'S MISSING LONDON HOME PINPOINTED AFTER CENTURIES-LONG MYSTERY, RESEARCHER SAYS

The identification involved nearly 30 years of collaboration between researchers and divers from the British Museum, Bournemouth University and the South West Maritime Archaeology Group.

Very little is known about the appearance or size of the Dom van Keulen and no known paintings of the ship exist, according to the Bournemouth University release.

The wreck site measures about 98 feet long and rests roughly 59 feet below the surface, where cannons, anchors and other artifacts are still scattered across the seabed.

Dave Parham, a professor of maritime archaeology at Bournemouth University and co-editor of the book, said the ship was carrying 150 bags of gum arabic, 64 bags of saltpeter, 320 goat skins and 9,000 Barbary ducats, or Moroccan gold coins.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"It is thought that most of the cargo was salvaged at the time, but more than 400 coins remained on the seabed until they were discovered by the South West Maritime Archaeology Group in 1995," Parham said in the press release.

Though he doesn't have a sense of how much the cargo would have been worth in the 17th century, Parham told Fox News Digital the ship was carrying what would now be considered a "general cargo" — a mix of everyday trade goods.

Parham said one of the most surprising aspects of the research was how ordinary most of the ship's cargo was — aside from the gold coins, which were crucial to the identification process.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

"It was the presence of the Moroccan coins, together with the dating evidence provided by the other finds, that allowed the historical research to be focused," he said.

He added that one of the most remarkable aspects of the discovery was that an international museum collaborated with a group of divers to reach "such a fantastic conclusion."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS

In a statement, British Museum Head of Research Jeremy D. Hill said it took years of collaboration among historians, archaeologists and divers to piece together the ship's history.

"The discovery of African gold from under the sea off the coast of Devon was an amazing find that raised so many questions about how it came to be there," Hill said, according to the Bournemouth University release.

"The story can now be told of how a Dutch ship carrying North African gold was wrecked off the English coast, making this a discovery of international importance. It reminds us how much there is still to be found under our seas."

item.title: Fetterman trolls the Democratic Socialists of America as Israel kills Hamas terrorists: 'Cry harder'
item.description: Sen. John Fetterman tagged the Democratic Socialists of America on X, telling them to "cry harder" as the Israel Defense Forces highlighted the elimination of several terrorists.
item.content:encoded:
Sen. John Fetterman, D-Pa., an ardent supporter of Israel, trolled the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) in a Thursday post on X.

"Israel keeps killing Hamas members. @DemSocialists, cry harder," he wrote, tagging the DSA.

In a Thursday post on X, the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) highlighted the elimination of several terrorists.

REPUBLICANS SAY THEY'D WELCOME FETTERMAN AS GOP EYES BIGGER SENATE MAJORITY

"ELIMINATED: Abdallah Jahja, a terrorist in the Naval Force of Hamas' military wing who infiltrated Israeli territory during the October 7 Massacre," the IDF's post stated.

The post added that "2 additional Hamas terrorists were eliminated," including "Mohammed Al-Hawari, a cell commander in Hamas' Zeitoun Battalion" and "Sabhi Saqallah, observation operative in Hamas’ Zeitoun Battalion."

"The terrorists advanced terror attacks targeting IDF troops & Israeli civilians," the IDF added.

SEN JOHN FETTERMAN PLEDGES TO BE 'LAST MAN STANDING' IN THE DEMOCRATIC PARTY IN SUPPORT OF ISRAEL

Fetterman told Fox News Channel's Kayleigh McEnany last week that "these DSA people are deeply anti-Israel" as well as "deeply anti-America."

The lawmaker said that he "will always be the last man standing within the Democratic caucus in the Senate that's pro-unapologetically standing for Israel."

"Israel is our special ally, and I'm always proud to stand with Israel," he said.

FETTERMAN REVEALS WHAT IT WOULD TAKE FOR HIM TO LEAVE DEMOCRATIC PARTY

Fetterman indicated last week during remarks at the Hill Nation Summit that he would "leave" the Democratic Party if it were ever to officially become the "anti-Israel party."

item.title: Hall of Famer Cris Carter says upcoming NFL season will be Travis Kelce’s ‘last dance’
item.description: Cris Carter's Instagram post with Travis Kelce sparked backlash after calling the tight end's upcoming season "The Last Dance" before Canton.
item.content:encoded:
Three-time Super Bowl champion Travis Kelce has already had a milestone summer away from football, but another major moment could be on the horizon.

Kelce confirmed in March that he would return for his 14th NFL season, putting months of retirement speculation to rest.

Not long after his July 3 wedding to singer Taylor Swift, Kelce appeared in an Instagram photo shared by Pro Football Hall of Famer Cris Carter, who added text across the image that quickly raised eyebrows.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

"Know the goat is preparing for THE LAST DANCE. Next stop Canton/HOF," Carter wrote on Monday.

Carter currently works as Florida Atlantic football’s director of player development, and the post referenced his years of work with Kelce. Andrew Spruill, Kelce’s longtime personal strength and conditioning coach, also appeared alongside them.

"Andrew and I started with Travis before his rookie year in the NFL," Carter wrote.

GREG OLSEN ON TRAVIS KELCE RETURNING TO CHIEFS FOR 14TH SEASON: 'LEAGUE IS BETTER OFF WITH HIM PLAYING'

Kelce’s offseason work also drew attention from Johnny Olsen, another trainer with extensive ties to the Chiefs star, as he prepares for the season kickoff.

Carter’s post sparked reaction on social media, with some fans questioning the reference to Kelce’s future.

"Can Travis be allowed to announce his own retirement, please? Everything about him makes headlines. Are you saying this for attention? He’s always said he doesn’t want a farewell tour or the focus to be on him during the season. Travis has earned the right to announce himself when he sees fit," one fan wrote in a social media post.

Kelce enters the 2026 campaign with more than 13,000 career regular-season receiving yards and 82 touchdowns.

The Chiefs' decade-long run of playoff appearances came to an abrupt end in Week 15 last season after they came up short at Arrowhead Stadium in a game against the Los Angeles Chargers.

Superstar quarterback Patrick Mahomes sustained a season-ending ACL injury when he scrambled to try and pick up some yards in the game’s final minutes. Mahomes participated in seven-on-seven drills in May, roughly five months removed from a procedure to address a torn ACL and LCL.

In January, Mahomes said he was aiming to be ready for the Chiefs’ regular-season opener.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: Jennifer Sey reveals alleged communication with Sophie Cunningham amid recruitment pitch for XX-XY Athletics
item.description: XX-XY Athletics CEO Jennifer Sey says she would "give my right arm" to sign Sophie Cunningham away from Adidas and make her the brand's face.
item.content:encoded:
Jennifer Sey is ready to make Sophie Cunningham the face of XX-XY Athletics — and says she would "give my right arm" to convince the WNBA star to leave Adidas for her brand.

Sey, the founder and CEO of the pro-women’s sportswear brand, told Fox News Digital she has wanted to sign Cunningham since the Indiana Fever star privately praised XX-XY Athletics’s viral "Dear Nike" campaign in 2024.

"She’s the perfect person for us," Sey said. "We have wanted to work with her for such a long time."

WHITE HOUSE DEFENDS WNBA STAR SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM AFTER SHE SPEAKS OUT IN SUPPORT OF PROTECTING WOMEN'S SPORTS

"It’s not just that she’s a fantastic athlete and she’s a currently competing professional athlete," she added. "She’s just got such an incredible personality. She’s fun and she’s funny and she’s tough and she says what she thinks, and that’s what we’re all about."

Sey revealed that she first opened communication with Cunningham after XX-XY Athletics released its "Dear Nike" in October 2024. The ad challenged the sportswear giant to take a public stand against biological males competing in women’s sports, and generated more than 10 million views.

Sey claims Cunningham liked and commented on the original ad, then sent Sey a private direct message on social media praising it. But Sey said she did not see the message for months because it landed in a folder for messages from accounts she did not follow.

FEVER GUARD SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM'S VIRAL FINGER-POINTING MEME REACHES WWE

"She liked it and commented on it. She actually wrote me a DM on Instagram, which I didn't see for months because I'm a moron and I never check the little tab from unknown people," Sey claims.

"Cause there's just so many in there, I just never look, and I didn't follow her. I didn't know who she was. And then a few months later, and she was kind of taking off at this point as Caitlin [Clark]'s defender... I checked that random tab from unknown DMs and she had DMed me about the ad, that she really liked the Dear Nike, and I was like, 'Oh my gosh, I should have... How did I miss this? I should have responded months ago.'"

Fox News Digital has reached out to Cunningham's representatives for comment.

Cunningham is already under contract with Adidas, which is preparing to release her first publicly available player-exclusive shoe Friday. The Adidas Crazy Energy "Sophie Cunningham" PE will retail for $120. It is a Cunningham colorway of an existing Adidas model, not a signature shoe created exclusively for her. Adidas recently unveiled the Crazy Energy line.

But Sey hopes Cunningham will eventually defect from Adidas.

"I would do anything within my power to get her to defect from Adidas and come to work with us," Sey said. "I don’t know that Sophie is likely to do that, but I would give my right arm to have her do it."

Sey criticized Adidas for not publicly endorsing Cunningham’s position in the days since the WNBA star made her position public.

"Adidas is not a brand that supports women," Sey said.

Sey also pointed to Adidas’ 2023 Pride campaign, which featured a male-presenting model wearing a swimsuit sold in the women’s section. Adidas said at the time that the collection promoted LGBTQIA+ advocacy and self-acceptance. Sey also criticized the company’s sponsorship of the Boston Marathon, whose rules permit transgender athletes to qualify in their gender category, although eligibility for prize money is governed by World Athletics standards.

"Even now, when she’s getting a lot of heat, it’s not like Adidas is coming out and saying, ‘We stand with Sophie. We agree women’s sports must be protected,’" Sey said. "They’re silent."

"They just want to capitalize on sassy Sophie who points," she added. "They don’t want to actually stand with her when she says something that makes people angry."

Still, Sey praised Adidas for not dropping Cunningham as a client since she's made her statements.

"It says something is shifting in the culture about this issue. They’re not dropping her," Sey said.

Sey conceded that XX-XY could not match the financial resources of Adidas. But she said her brand could offer Cunningham something the global sportswear company could not: top billing and unconditional support.

Sey previously served as Levi Strauss & Co.’s chief marketing officer and global brand president. She said she personally negotiated celebrity partnerships while at Levi’s and often sold potential ambassadors on the relationship rather than the biggest possible check.

"I wasn’t offering the most money," Sey said. "I was offering the best partnership."

Asked how much she would offer Cunningham, Sey declined to name a figure.

"I would pull together as much money as I could to pay her, to make her a favorable deal, but I’m not going to pretend like I can pay what Adidas can pay," Sey said. "We’re going to be more fun to work with."

"She’s not going to be a third-rate athlete on our roster. She’s going to be number one," Sey added. "And she’s going to be able to say whatever she wants, and we’re going to have her back."

Sey is also pitching Cunningham on something bigger than a traditional endorsement.

After the WNBA Players Association declined to allow its group licensees to commercialize Cunningham’s viral pointing image, Sey posted a silhouette modeled on the pose. The union called the image "derogatory," according to an initial report, but later explained that it generally avoids monetizing confrontations between its members. It clarified that Cunningham remains free to license or create her own products.

Sey offered to produce a hoodie featuring the silhouette and give Cunningham all the proceeds. She now envisions using it as the logo for a Cunningham line under XX-XY, similar to how Michael Jordan’s Jumpman pose became the symbol of Jordan Brand.

"Sophie can have her own brand like Jordan with us, and that’s the logo," Sey said.

"That should be the logo for the XX-XY Cunningham collection."

Cunningham’s profile has risen sharply since she joined Caitlin Clark and the Fever in 2025. She became known as Clark’s on-court defender, then turned into one of the WNBA’s most recognizable personalities after her prolonged finger-pointing exchange with Phoenix Mercury veteran DeWanna Bonner went viral in June.

The 29-year-old guard is also enjoying one of her most efficient seasons. Cunningham entered the All-Star break averaging 9.3 points while shooting 47.4% from the field and 42.9% from 3-point range.

Her growing popularity has extended beyond basketball. Cunningham recently appeared as a guest ring girl at UFC 329 and has built her image around an outspoken, humorous and combative personality.

She then waded into one of the most divisive issues in women’s sports.

Cunningham told ESPN this week that she wanted to protect girls who "shouldn’t have to go against biological men" in sports or share locker rooms with them. She later doubled down, calling her position "common sense" while insisting she does not hate transgender people.

"I said what I said," Cunningham told reporters Wednesday. "I think it’s really important to protect children, and that’s little girls who are also involved in that category. I stand on what I said."

The Fever responded by saying its players have the right to their own views.

"Our players are thoughtful adults with their own perspectives and voices, and those views are their own," the organization said. "We are committed to welcoming fans from every background and treating everyone with respect, and that’s what guides us as an organization."
